title: Are the Packers better than the Bears?
text:
The Green Bay Packers and the Chicago Bears have been known rivals for over a century, which is one of the oldest rivalries in the NFL. The first game they played against each other, the Bears won 20-0 in 1921. Since then, they have played over 200 games against each other, which is also the most-played matchup.
This season, the Packers lost to the Bears two out of the three times they faced head-to-head, which made Packers fans everywhere question if they are the superior team. With several player injuries this season from the Packers, including the star quarterback Jordan Love, it left the fans with a sour taste in their mouth watching the Bears come back from being down with only minutes left in the fourth quarter.
Sports Reference gave numbers for the past 10 years showing the Simple Rating System of all the NFC teams, including teams that have moved from cities and changed names. The SRS rates teams by combining the average rate differential and strength of schedule, measuring how many points better or worse a team is than the average team (0.0).


Code
library(tidyverse)
nfc <- read_csv("nfcstandings.csv")
srs <- nfc |> 
  group_by(Team) |> 
  summarize(
    total_SRS = sum(SRS)
  )

cb <- nfc |> 
  group_by(Team) |> 
  summarize(
    total_SRS = sum(SRS)
  ) |> 
  filter(Team == "Chicago Bears")


gb<- nfc |> 
  group_by(Team) |> 
  summarize(
    total_SRS = sum(SRS)
  ) |> 
  filter(Team == "Green Bay Packers")

ggplot() +
  geom_bar(data = srs, aes(x=reorder(Team, total_SRS), weight = total_SRS), fill = "lightgrey") +
  geom_bar(data = gb, aes(x=reorder(Team, total_SRS), weight = total_SRS), fill = "#203731")+
  geom_bar(data = cb, aes(x=reorder(Team, total_SRS), weight = total_SRS), fill = "#C83803") +
  coord_flip()+ 
  labs(
    x="Team", 
    y="Total SRS", 
    title="Where Each NFC Team Stands in the SRS", 
    subtitle="The Packers fall in 3rd for the best SRS in the NFC in the past 10 years while the Bears fall 3rd to last and \nlast in the NFC North. The Seattle Seahawks and Los Angeles Rams rank ahead of the Packers.", 
    caption="Source: Sports Reference | By Avery Regier"
    ) +
  theme_minimal()+
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )









Looking at the rating, the Packers, in the past 10 years, have shown a far superior SRS than the Bears and most of the NFC, ranking third highest. While the Bears are ranking third to last. This shows that they have been playing better than not just the Bears, but most of the NFC.
To dive deeper into the NFC, specifically looking at the NFC North, where do the Packers stand offensively?
Yardage is a crucial part of football, not only toward getting touchdowns, but also in determining how many you can get in a play and an attempt. Have the Packers had overall better yardage in the past 10 years, and how do they stand in the NFC North?


Code
offense <- read_csv("nfloffstats.csv")
gb <- offense |> 
  filter(Tm == "Green Bay Packers") |> 
  summarize(
    total_ypp = sum(yardsperplay),
    total_ya = sum(yardsperattempt)
  )

cb <- offense |> 
  filter(Tm == "Chicago Bears") |> 
  summarize(
    total_ypp = sum(yardsperplay),
    total_ya = sum(yardsperattempt)
  )

nfcypp <- offense |> 
  filter(Tm == c("Green Bay Packers", "Chicago Bears", "Detroit Lions", "Minnesota Vikings")) |> 
  group_by(Tm) |> 
  summarize(
    total_ypp = sum(yardsperplay),
    total_ya = sum(yardsperattempt)
  )
ggplot() +
  geom_segment(
    data=nfcypp,
    aes(
      y= reorder(Tm, total_ypp), yend=Tm,
      x=total_ypp, xend=total_ya),
    color="lightgrey",
    linewidth = 1.2
  )+ 
  geom_point(
    data=nfcypp,
    aes(
      y=Tm,
      x=total_ypp,
      color = "Yards Per Play"
    ), size=2
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=nfcypp,
    aes(
      y=Tm,
      x=total_ya,
      color="Yards Per Attempt"
    ), size = 2
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(name="Total YPP to YA", values=c("red", "blue")) + 
  labs(
    x="Yards Per Play Compared to Yards Per Attempt", 
    y="Team", 
    title="The Packers Lead in Yardage in the NFC North", 
    subtitle="The Packers have a great lead over the Chicago Bears in yardage over the past 10 years.\nThe Bears fall in last in the NFC North, while the Packers lead the division followed by the Vikings.", 
    caption="Source: Sports Reference | By Avery Regier"
    ) +
  theme_minimal()+
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )









The Packers are leading the charge in not only the yards per play, but also the yards per attempt, showing a strong offense for the team. Now look at where the Bears fall, dead last in the division. Looks like they have some offensive work to do, although these last two matchups against the Packers may show a different case.
Broadening the scale, let’s look at the NFL as a whole. Comparing every team to these rivals.
Turnovers can be a game-changer. Especially in those last crucial minutes, fans are white-knuckling their beverages, and their eyes are locked on the screen. Watching your quarterback throw an interception is gut-wrenching to any football fan, so let’s see what the past 10 years look like for the Packers and the Bears’ turnover percentage.


Code
defense<- read_csv("nfldefensivestats.csv")
gbay <- defense |> 
  filter(Tm == "Green Bay Packers")
cbears <- defense |> 
  filter(Tm == "Chicago Bears")
ggplot()+
  geom_line(data=defense, aes(x=Season, y=TOpercent, group=Tm), color="lightgrey")+
  geom_line(data=gbay, aes(x=Season, y=TOpercent, group=Tm), color="#203731") +
  geom_line(data=cbears, aes(x=Season, y=TOpercent, group=Tm), color="#C83803")+
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0,20))+ 
  labs(
    x="Season", 
    y="Turnover Percentage", 
    title="The Bears Can't Keep the Ball", 
    subtitle="The Bears have had a more fluctuating turnover percentage than the Packers in the past 10 years. ", 
    caption="Source: Sports Reference | By Avery Regier"
    ) +
  theme_minimal()+
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )









The Bears had a rough streak in the 2018 season, peaking at the top of the NFL in turnover percentage, which isn’t necessarily what you want to be at the top of. Their percentage has fluctuated over the years, while the Packers have stayed pretty consistent with a downward slope by the end of the 2025 season.
After this season, it was hard to be a Packers fan, but it is good to know that historically, the Packers have always been better than the Bears. With the draft bringing in fresh bodies, it leaves fans with the question of how the team will look next year. For them, hopefully better than the Bears.
